root_cause: No production fault existed — this was a false alarm. The web service (calm-svc release stable-1.4.2) was and remained fully healthy throughout the investigation. Independent probing showed 100/100 successful requests against the load path (/orders), p99 latency ~3.9ms, median ~1.7ms, max ~4.8ms, with /healthz returning 200. The container had RestartCount=0, was not OOM-killed, logged no errors, and the load generator reported all requests succeeding. The p99 latency page was therefore a spurious/already-recovered blip or a misconfigured alert threshold rather than an actual latency regression. There was no recent release or configuration change to the web service to explain a regression.
actions_taken: Investigation only; no changes were made to any service. Steps: (1) enumerated containers with docker ps/stats — all up, CPU <1%, memory a few MiB, no resource pressure; (2) inspected logs for web, load, payments, and operator — web reported healthy, load reported all requests succeeding, no errors anywhere; (3) mapped endpoints and probed latency from the host, first a small sample then a 100-request sample against /orders, observing 0 non-200 responses and single-digit-millisecond p99; (4) reviewed docker-compose configuration confirming stable-1.4.2 with no recent change; (5) inspected the web container state (RestartCount=0, OOMKilled=false). Concluded the alert was a false alarm and deliberately made no remediation changes. The unrelated healthy 'payments' neighbor was not touched.
postmortem: Blameless summary: A p99 latency monitor paged for the web service. On investigation the service was healthy by every available signal — sub-5ms p99, zero error rate, no restarts, no resource pressure, and a steady succeeding load stream — so no remediation was warranted and none was performed. The key on-call lesson is that not every page requires action; the least-risky and correct response to a healthy system is to change nothing, avoiding the risk of an unnecessary restart or config change causing a real outage (especially near the unrelated payments neighbor). Follow-up recommendations for the monitoring owners: (1) review the p99 alert threshold and evaluation window/for-duration to reduce single-blip false positives; (2) require a minimum sample count / sustained breach before paging; (3) confirm the alert query targets the correct service and percentile; (4) consider adding auto-resolve context so responders can quickly see whether the condition already recovered. No customer impact occurred.
